name: Scandium
Analysis:
The 3d transition series has one of the most irregular electron gain enthalpy trends in the periodic table. Instead of a smooth pattern, the values are shaped by a tug-of-war between exchange energy, electron pairing, d-orbital stability, and the steadily increasing effective nuclear charge.

Scandium and titanium show only slightly negative values because the added electron provides little extra stabilization. Vanadium and especially chromium gain electrons more readily as the added electron increases exchange energy, with chromium approaching the highly stable half-filled 3d⁵ configuration.

Manganese is the major exception. Its 3d⁵4s² configuration is already exceptionally stable, so adding an electron disrupts the half-filled subshell, giving it an electron gain enthalpy close to zero.

From iron to nickel, the electron gain enthalpy becomes progressively more negative. Although electron pairing increases, the rising effective nuclear charge and poor shielding by the 3d electrons strengthen the attraction for the incoming electron, outweighing the unfavorable pairing effects.

Copper has one of the most negative electron gain enthalpies because gaining an electron forms the highly stable 3d¹⁰4s² configuration. Zinc, however, ends the series with a value close to zero, as its already stable 3d¹⁰4s² configuration forces the extra electron into the higher-energy 4p orbital.

name: Yttrium
Analysis:
The 4d transition series is full of peaks and dips, with electron gain enthalpy controlled by the interplay of exchange energy, electron pairing, and effective nuclear charge.

From yttrium to zirconium, the values become more negative as increasing effective nuclear charge and the gain in exchange energy make electron gain more favorable. Niobium reaches a major peak because gaining an electron produces the exceptionally stable half-filled 4d⁵ configuration.

Molybdenum and technetium reverse the trend. Adding an electron disrupts the 4d⁵ configuration, reducing exchange energy, while technetium also experiences additional electron pairing, making electron gain less favorable.

The values become more negative again for ruthenium and rhodium, where the increasing effective nuclear charge outweighs the effects of electron pairing. Palladium then shows a sharp drop because its filled 4d¹⁰ subshell is already highly stable, forcing the extra electron into the higher-energy 5p orbital.

Silver has the most negative electron gain enthalpy in the series as electron gain forms the stable 4d¹⁰5s² configuration, while cadmium ends the trend with a value close to zero because the incoming electron must occupy the higher-energy 5p subshell.

name: Hafnium
Analysis:
The 5d transition series displays one of the most fascinating electron gain enthalpy trends, shaped by exchange energy, lanthanide contraction, and powerful relativistic effects.

From hafnium to tungsten, the electron gain enthalpy becomes increasingly negative as the effective nuclear charge rises and the added electron increases exchange energy, with tungsten approaching the stable half-filled 5d⁵ configuration.

Rhenium breaks the trend. Gaining an electron disrupts the 5d⁵ configuration and introduces electron pairing, making electron gain much less favorable.

From osmium to platinum, the values become more negative again as the increasing effective nuclear charge overcomes the unfavorable effects of electron pairing. Gold reaches the most negative electron gain enthalpy in the series because gaining an electron forms the highly stable 5d¹⁰6s² configuration, while strong relativistic stabilization of the 6s orbital makes electron gain exceptionally favorable.

Mercury ends the series with a value close to zero. Its already stable 5d¹⁰6s² configuration forces the incoming electron into the higher-energy 6p orbital, making electron gain energetically unfavorable.

name: Rutherfordium
Analysis:
The 6d transition series remains one of the greatest frontiers of modern chemistry. Reliable electron gain enthalpy values for rutherfordium (Rf) to copernicium (Cn) are unavailable because these superheavy elements are produced atom-by-atom and exist for only fractions of a second.

Theoretical studies suggest that their electron gain enthalpies are governed by the same factors as the lighter transition metals—effective nuclear charge, exchange energy, electron pairing, and the stability of half-filled and fully filled d-subshells.

But there is a crucial difference. In the 6d series, relativistic effects become dominant. The enormous nuclear charge reshapes the 6d and 7s orbitals, changing their energies and even their ordering. As a result, these superheavy elements are expected to deviate significantly from the familiar trends observed in the 3d, 4d, and 5d transition series, making them one of the most fascinating—and least explored—regions of the periodic table.
